name: Boxal
classification_notes: A pixel-style display font drawing directly from arcade game bitmap aesthetics, with five decorative style variants that place it firmly in the display category rather than any conventional text classification.
summary: A pixel display font with five style variants drawing on arcade game graphics from the 8-bit era.
description: Constructed on a pixel grid with explicit reference to classic arcade game typography, Boxal comes in five visual variants: Normal, Diamond, Dot, Square and Line. The family spans 15 fonts with over 610 characters, including 10 number variations, alternate lowercase a and g, arrows and emojis. Language support covers Western, Southern and Central European Latin.
foundry_blurb: The Northern Block describe Boxal as a pixel font inspired by the enduring charm of classic arcade gaming, with each letterform designed to evoke memories of pixelated adventures. The five style variants, Normal, Diamond, Dot, Square and Line, offer a range of interpretations of classic pixel artistry, and the foundry position it for both digital and print work wherever a retro gaming aesthetic is required.

name: Syke Mono Slab Rounded
classification_notes: A monospaced typeface with slab serif construction and rounded terminals; the combination of fixed-width rhythm, slab serifs and softened curves places it firmly in the slab mono subcategory.
summary: A monospaced slab serif with rounded terminals, available in five weights with italics for coding, editorial and branding work.
description: Fixed-width construction meets slab serif structure with softened, rounded terminals that reduce the severity typical of monospaced designs. The family runs to 755 characters per style and includes practical OpenType features: two alternates for the lowercase a, variants for g, i, l and r, ten numeral styles, fractions, case-sensitive punctuation, slashed zero and localised forms. The combination of strict monospaced rhythm and warmer detailing gives it range across code editors, editorial layouts and identity applications.
foundry_blurb: The Northern Block describe Syke Mono Slab Rounded as a contemporary monospaced typeface that blends the discipline of slab serifs with softened rounded detailing. They highlight its precise, consistent geometry offset by curved terminals that bring warmth and approachability, noting the balance between engineered clarity and visual comfort as making it equally suited to coding environments, editorial use and expressive branding.

name: Corbert Text
classification_notes: Corbert Text is described as having a distinctive geometric character, consistent with a geometric sans classification. It is an optical text variant of the display-oriented Corbert, refined for small sizes.
summary: An optical text variant of Corbert, scaled and refined for sizes 9pt and below across eight weights with matching italics and a semi-condensed style.
description: Introduced as a companion to the display-oriented Corbert (2013), Corbert Text is a full optical reworking rather than a simple x-height adjustment, with the proportions and spacing tuned for small-size legibility while retaining the original's geometric character. The family spans eight weights from Thin to ExtraBold, each with a matching italic, plus a semi-condensed style for space-restricted layouts. Over 935 characters per style cover Western, Central and Eastern European Latin, with OpenType features including small caps, stylistic alternates, tabular figures, fractions and circled numbers.
foundry_blurb: The Northern Block describe Corbert Text as an optical variant of Corbert, designed for use at 9pt and below. Rather than simply increasing the x-height, the entire typeface was scaled and refined to improve legibility at small sizes while preserving Corbert's geometric character. The addition of a semi-condensed weight makes it suited to editorial and complex UI work.

name: Selestin
classification_notes: Selestin is a high-contrast display serif with calligraphic origins and engraved letterform references, placing it firmly in the display category with a classical decorative serif subcategory informed by 18th-century engraving and Art Nouveau.
summary: A high-contrast display serif drawing on 18th-century engraving and broad-nib calligraphy, with ball terminals and delicate serifs across 18 styles.
description: Built from references to engraved typography, classic calligraphy and Art Nouveau ornament, Selestin offers 681 characters per style with a full suite of OpenType features including contextual alternates, discretionary ligatures and 14 stylistic sets. Four numeral styles, lining through oldstyle, add typographic flexibility for demanding editorial and packaging applications. The family sits naturally in luxury branding and high-end print where visible craft and formal elegance are priorities.
foundry_blurb: The Northern Block describe Selestin as a high-contrast display serif combining the fluid elegance of calligraphy with the sharp precision of engraved typography. They cite 18th-century engraving, broad-nib calligraphy and Art Nouveau ornamentation as its inspirations, and position it for editorial design, luxury branding and high-end packaging. The complete family spans 18 fonts with 681 characters per style and extensive OpenType features.

name: Pennline Script
classification_notes: A semi-connected brush-inspired script reviving the 1899 Bulletin typeface from Keystone Type Foundry; the design draws on American hand-lettering traditions with lively, irregular strokes characteristic of brush script.
summary: A digitised revival of Bulletin (1899, Keystone Type Foundry) redrawn as a semi-connected brush script with extensive OpenType features for expressive typographic control.
description: Rooted in American hand-lettering tradition, the design preserves the expressive irregularity of its 1899 source while adapting glyphs for contemporary use across headlines, packaging and editorial. Over 1,050 characters cover 304 Latin-based languages, and eight stylistic sets alongside contextual alternates, multiple ligature sets and initial, medial and terminal forms give designers considerable room to vary its texture. The approachable vintage character suits projects seeking warmth and nostalgia without abandoning legibility.
foundry_blurb: The Northern Block describe Pennline Script as a digitised revival of Bulletin, first cast in 1899 by the Keystone Type Foundry of Philadelphia. They note its semi-connected, brush-inspired design balances historical authenticity with modern functionality, and highlight its 1,050-plus characters, 304 Latin-based language support and extensive OpenType features including contextual alternates, ligatures, localised forms and eight stylistic sets.

name: Lintel Next
classification_notes: Explicitly described as a geometric sans-serif inspired by architectural principles, featuring pill-shaped curves and carefully proportioned letterforms that balance structure with a flowing aesthetic.
summary: An evolution of The Northern Block's 2012 Lintel, redrawn across six widths with Greek and Vietnamese support added under Tasos Varipatis.
description: Originally released in 2012, this geometric sans has been substantially expanded and redrawn, with five new widths running from Compressed to Extended now sitting alongside the standard cut. The hand-drawn approach of Tasos Varipatis softens the geometric rigidity common to the genre, introducing pill-shaped curves that keep the letterforms approachable without losing their structural precision. Coverage extends to Western, Southern and Central European Latin, Cyrillic, Greek and Vietnamese, across a 1222-character set with broad OpenType feature support.
foundry_blurb: The Northern Block describe Lintel Next as the next-generation evolution of the original Lintel typeface, inspired by the architectural principles of Alvar Aalto. The update, led by Tasos Varipatis, redraws every character for improved consistency and adds five new widths, Greek and Vietnamese support, and extensive OpenType features across a 96-font family suited to branding, editorial and UI design.

name: Celdum
classification_notes: A square, mono-line geometric sans built on a rectangular grid with rounded corners and ink-traps; its engineered, mechanical character places it firmly in the geometric sans category.
summary: A square geometric sans built on a rectangular grid, with ink-traps and rounded corners giving it a controlled mechanical character suited to branding, signage and screen work.
description: Constructed on a strict rectangular grid, Celdum balances smooth curves against hard verticals and horizontals in a mono-line design that reads as both engineered and approachable. Version 2.0 introduces ink-traps for improved small-size clarity and a more industrial edge, while the boldest weight has been reinforced for headline impact. Eight weights run from Thin to Black with matching obliques, each containing over 600 glyphs and a broad set of OpenType features including multiple numeral styles, fractions and stylistic sets.
foundry_blurb: The Northern Block describe Celdum as a square sans serif shaped by a rectangular grid, balancing smooth curves with strict verticals and horizontals in a mono-line design that feels engineered yet approachable. Version 2.0 adds ink-traps for a more industrial character and better legibility at small sizes, with a reinforced Bold for display use. The family is positioned as a versatile toolkit for branding, mobile apps, video games, signage and interactive media.

name: Schar
classification_notes: The foundry explicitly classifies Schar as a humanist sans serif, noting its angled axis and the balance and rhythm associated with serif design stripped back for contemporary use.
summary: A humanist sans with serif-like rhythm and an angled axis, offered in eight weights with true italics and extensive OpenType support.
description: Schar 2.0 draws its structure from humanist serif proportions while dispensing with the serifs themselves, producing a sans with fluid curves and a dynamic angled axis. The 16-font family covers eight weights from Thin to Black with true italics, over 1,000 characters, true small caps, and thirteen number styles ranging from tabular to circled figures. Alternative glyphs for a, e, g, y and l, plus ligatures and a full OpenType feature set, give it enough flexibility for branding, editorial and interface work.
foundry_blurb: The Northern Block describe Schar 2.0 as a humanist sans serif designed with the balance and rhythm of a serif but stripped back for a modern, versatile feel. Its angled axis and refined curves give it a fluid, dynamic personality, while improved spacing and clarity make it suitable for both print and digital settings. The family includes eight weights with true italics and over 1,000 characters, along with practical features such as alternative letters, thirteen number styles, true small caps and an extensive set of OpenType features.

name: Werkhaus
classification_notes: Geometric, near-monoline letterforms with Bauhaus and retro gaming influences place this firmly in the display category; the oblique rather than true italic, bold aesthetic and cartridge-packaging inspiration confirm it as a geometric display rather than a general-purpose sans.
summary: A near-monoline geometric display family drawing on Bauhaus 93 and Atari 2600 cartridge graphics, available in seven weights with matching obliques.
description: The almost-monoline construction references both Bauhaus geometry and the bold, punchy lettering found on Atari 2600 game cartridges, giving it a retro-inflected character that sits between mid-century constructivism and 1980s pop graphics. Seven weights run from Thin to Black, each with an oblique companion, and twenty stylistic alternates introduce a softer, rounded option for added range. Over 500 characters with eight numeral variations and broad European language support make it a practical choice for display-scale branding and editorial work.
foundry_blurb: Werkhaus is described by The Northern Block as a modern display typeface inspired by Bauhaus 93 and the bold aesthetics of Atari 2600 cartridge packaging, combining geometric simplicity with dynamic retro influences and an almost-monoline structure. The family spans seven weights with matching obliques, includes twenty interchangeable stylistic alternates for a softer rounded look, and offers over 500 characters with eight numeral variations and extensive South, East and Western European language support.

name: Dohrma
classification_notes: Dohrma is a modernised take on Morris Fuller Benton's Othello, a high-contrast inline display typeface. Its slender letterforms with strong vertical strokes and decorative inline/outline treatments place it firmly in the display category.
summary: A contemporary reworking of Morris Fuller Benton's Othello, offered in four stylistic variants suited to large-scale display work.
description: Built on the bones of Benton's high-contrast Othello, this typeface updates the original's slender, dramatic letterforms with four distinct treatments: Black, Outline, Soft and Grit. Each style ships with over 550 characters and a range of OpenType alternates for the letters N, Y, a, j, r, t and y. Coverage extends across Western, Southern and Central European Latin, making it a practical choice for packaging, posters and film credits.
foundry_blurb: The Northern Block describe Dohrma as a modernised rendition of the classic Othello typeface, originally conceived by Morris Fuller Benton in 1964. Its slender and bold letterforms marry curves with straight lines, making it suited to books, packaging, posters and film credits, particularly in large display scenarios. The family comprises four distinctive styles — Black, Outline, Soft and Grit — each with over 550 characters and OpenType features including case-sensitive forms, standard ligatures and language support for Western, Southern and Central Europe.

name: Zaius
classification_notes: A bold, decorative sans-serif with stencil and outline variants, inspired by 1960s movie lettering; its expressive weight and stylistic variants place it firmly in the display category.
summary: A bold display sans inspired by Ed Benguiat's lettering for Planet of the Apes (1968), updated with stencil, outline and fill variants.
description: Version 2.0 of this display typeface refines the heavy, expressive aesthetic of the original with expanded character support across Western, South and Central European languages, plus eight numeral variations. The family comprises four styles: the base weight alongside Ultra, Stencil and Outline cuts, making it well suited to print, motion graphics and apparel. Its lineage in 1960s sci-fi movie titling gives it a period flavour that works for retro and editorial contexts.
foundry_blurb: The Northern Block describe Zaius 2.0 as a bold sans-serif inspired by Ed Benguiat's iconic lettering for Planet of the Apes (1968), updated with technical refinements, over 500 characters, eight numeral variations, and new stylistic variants including Ultra, Stencil, Outline and Fill, with language support covering Western, South and Central Europe.

name: Loew Next Devanagari
classification_notes: Loew Next is a geometric sans-serif; this Devanagari extension maintains the same geometric precision and modular construction across both Latin and Devanagari scripts.
summary: A Devanagari extension of Loew Next that adapts its geometric Latin construction to accommodate Hindi, Marathi and Sanskrit scripts within a single cohesive family.
description: Eight fonts bringing Devanagari support to the geometric sans Loew Next, with consistent weight progression and visual rhythm across both scripts. The extension is intended for multi-script settings where Latin and Devanagari text must coexist without tonal disruption. Readability across both scripts is the stated priority, making it a practical choice for international publishing, branding and digital interfaces.
foundry_blurb: The Northern Block describe Loew Next Devanagari as an extension of Loew Next that incorporates the Devanagari script for languages including Hindi, Marathi and Sanskrit. They note it adapts the geometric precision and modernity of Loew Next to the intricate forms of Devanagari, maintaining visual consistency and readability across both scripts to broaden usability for a global audience.

name: Scriber
classification_notes: Scriber is explicitly described as a geometric stencil typeface, characterised by deliberate stroke breaks and sharp angles that give it an industrial, technical quality.
summary: A six-weight geometric stencil face with deliberate stroke breaks that give it a clean, industrial character suited to technical and editorial contexts.
description: Built around straight lines, sharp angles and intentional gaps in the letterforms, Scriber leans into the stencil genre without sacrificing legibility or consistency. Six weights carry over 500 characters each, with a thorough set of OpenType features including tabular figures, fractions, ligatures and case-sensitive forms. Western, Southern and Central European Latin scripts are covered, making it a practical choice for multilingual projects alongside its typographic character.
foundry_blurb: The Northern Block describe Scriber as a meticulously crafted geometric stencil typeface that merges precision with contemporary aesthetics. They position it for both digital and print media, highlighting its clean straight lines, sharp angles and distinctive stroke breaks that lend an industrial flair to modern, technical projects.

name: Nurom Next
classification_notes: Described by the foundry as a contemporary sans-serif influenced by early grotesque styles, avoiding strict historical revival in favour of a fresh interpretation of the grotesque tradition.
summary: A contemporary grotesque evolved from the 2017 Nurom typeface, now spanning 20 weights with broad multilingual support across Latin, Cyrillic, Greek, Hebrew and Vietnamese.
description: An update to The Northern Block's original Nurom, this release draws on early grotesque conventions while avoiding strict historical revival in favour of clean lines and balanced proportions. The family ships with over 1,000 characters and a comprehensive set of OpenType features including scientific forms, tabular and proportional figures, case forms and discretionary ligatures. Extended language support across Cyrillic, Greek, Hebrew and Vietnamese makes it a practical choice for internationally facing work.
foundry_blurb: The Northern Block describe Nurom Next as the evolution of the original Nurom released in 2017, a contemporary sans-serif influenced by early grotesque styles that prioritises clean lines, balanced proportions and a fresh personality over strict historical revival. The update adds Cyrillic, Greek, Hebrew and Vietnamese support, bringing the character set to over 1,000 glyphs across 20 weights with a wide range of OpenType options.

name: Neogram
classification_notes: Explicitly described as a neo-grotesque rooted in Swiss design principles, with an original design reference to Robert Büchler's Typographie (1960), placing it firmly in the Swiss modernist grotesque tradition.
summary: A Swiss-rooted neo-grotesque expanded in 2024 to 90 fonts across five widths, from Condensed to Expanded.
description: Originally released in 2012 and referencing Robert Büchler's 1960 Typographie, Neogram has grown into a large working family with a 2024 update introducing SemiCondensed and Wide alongside the existing Normal, Condensed and Expanded widths. Weight distribution has shifted from a mathematical approach to a hand-eye method, intended to produce more visually even results across the full range. OpenType features include circle and squared numbers, directional arrows, tabular figures, fractions, ligatures and case-sensitive forms, with coverage across Western, Southern and Central European languages.
foundry_blurb: The Northern Block describe Neogram as a modern neo-grotesque type family rooted in Swiss design principles, originally released in 2012 and significantly updated in 2024. The update adds two new widths — SemiCondensed and Wide — bringing the family to 90 fonts, and introduces a more organic hand-eye approach to weight distribution in place of the earlier mathematical method. The design traces back to Robert Büchler's Typographie of 1960.

name: Gelder Sans
classification_notes: Inspired by Frutiger, the typeface sits between grotesque and humanist categories, combining open apertures with organic shapes and uniform stroke widths — characteristics that align most closely with the humanist sans tradition.
summary: A Frutiger-inspired humanist sans with open apertures and organic shapes, offered in eight weights with italics and extensive language support.
description: Drawing from Frutiger's legibility-first principles, Gelder Sans blends grotesque structure with humanist warmth to produce a typeface suited to both screen and print. The family spans eight weights with matching italics across 16 fonts in total, and includes over 1,000 characters covering Western, Central and South European scripts as well as Vietnamese. Alternate characters, small caps, ligatures and ten numeral variants give designers room to work at a typographic level beyond the basic.
foundry_blurb: The Northern Block describe Gelder Sans as a modern sans-serif inspired by the iconic Frutiger, named in honour of the designer's grandparents and their Dutch heritage. They position it as occupying a space between grotesque and humanist styles, taking the best elements of both, and highlight its open apertures, organic shapes and uniform stroke widths for readability. The family includes eight weights and italics, over 1,000 characters, alternate glyphs, small caps, ligatures and support for Western, Central and South European languages as well as Vietnamese.

name: Heltar
classification_notes: Explicitly described as a neo-grotesque, drawing from Helvetica and Haas Unica lineage with ink traps added for a machine-like aesthetic. Sits squarely in the neo-grotesque tradition with optical corrections and tight-tracking readability.
summary: A neo-grotesque with Helvetica and Haas Unica roots, updated with ink traps for a machine-like, technology-facing aesthetic.
description: Heltar takes its cues from Helvetica and Haas Unica, adding optical corrections for tight tracking and ink traps that give it a stylised, mechanical edge. Eight weights with matching obliques are included, alongside over 650 characters per style, fourteen number variants and alternate glyphs for characters such as I, R, a, g and l. Language support covers Western, Southern and Central European Latin scripts, making it a broad-ranging choice for tech and manufacturing branding.
foundry_blurb: The Northern Block describe Heltar as a modern neo-grotesque born from Helvetica's legacy and refined for the digital age, citing inspiration from The Designers Republic's treatment of Helvetica and the improvements of Haas Unica. They position ink traps as a deliberate choice for a machine-like aesthetic suited to brands in manufacturing and technology seeking a sleek, futuristic character.

name: Mynor-B
classification_notes: Mynor-B is described as a departure from the geometric design of Mynor towards a more humanist, expressive style with organic forms, placing it firmly in the humanist sans-serif subcategory.
summary: A humanist reworking of the geometric Mynor, introducing organic forms and expressive details to improve readability in extended text.
description: Where Mynor leaned on squarish geometric construction, Mynor-B introduces organic, humanist forms inspired by the Japanese art of Kintsugi, aiming for greater warmth and legibility in longer reads. The family spans seven weights with matching obliques and over 950 characters per style, including fourteen number variations, alternate lowercase characters, small caps and arrows. Language support covers Western, Southern and Central Europe as well as Vietnamese.
foundry_blurb: The Northern Block describe Mynor-B as a deliberate shift away from the squarish geometric design of its predecessor Mynor, towards a more humanist, expressive and readable typeface suited to longer texts. Inspired by Kintsugi, it introduces organic forms and expressive elements, balancing its geometric origins with a human touch. The family includes seven weights and obliques, over 950 characters per style, fourteen number variations, alternate lowercase characters, small caps and broad European plus Vietnamese language support.

name: Mynor
classification_notes: Mynor draws on OCR-A and OCR-B machine-readable typefaces of the 1950s, combining squarish geometric construction with smooth humanist curves. The blend of digital straight lines and rounded contours places it firmly in the geometric sans category with a distinct technical inflection.
summary: A squarish geometric sans drawing on OCR-A and OCR-B, available in seven weights and six widths with 84 styles in total.
description: Built around the machine-readable typefaces of the 1950s, Mynor pairs the clean rectilinear forms of OCR-A and OCR-B with smoothed, humanist curves to soften the technical edge. Seven weights run from Thin to Black, each with a matching italic, across six widths from Condensed to ExtraWide. Each style carries 450 characters with five numeral variants and full OpenType support for Western, Southern and Central European languages.
foundry_blurb: The Northern Block describe Mynor as a modern squarish sans inspired by machine-readable typefaces of the 1950s, including OCR-A and B. They note that smooth curved contours with a humanist touch sit alongside pure digital straight lines, with the contrasting shapes creating a modern aesthetic suited to both visual and computer-readable applications. The family includes seven weights with matching italics, six widths, 450 characters per style, five numeral variants and broad European language support.

name: Markus Low
classification_notes: Based on Basilea (1965), the design draws on classical romanesque inscribed letterforms with high stroke contrast and chisel-like details, positioning it as a contemporary display serif with historical roots.
summary: A high-contrast display serif reviving the inscribed forms of Markus Low's 1965 Basilea, updated across eight weights with obliques and extensive OpenType support.
description: Rooted in the classical romanesque letterforms of Basilea, originally drawn by Markus Low in 1965, this revival steers the design toward a contemporary register without losing the chisel-like tension of its source material. Eight weights run from Thin to Ultra, each paired with a matching oblique, giving the family range across headline and display settings. OpenType features include small caps, ten numeral variants, 14 alternates, and coverage of Western, Southern and Central European Latin.
foundry_blurb: The Northern Block describe Markus Low as a re-imagined inscribed serif based on Basilea, originally created by designer Markus Low in 1965. They note its great stroke contrast and graceful, fluid lines, and position it as a modern display serif with subtle chisel-like details suited for dramatic impact. Version 2.0 includes over 780 characters per style, eight weights with matching obliques, and extensive OpenType features including small caps, multiple numeral styles and broad European language support.

name: Zabawa
classification_notes: Zabawa is a handwritten brush script drawn with a pointed brush tool, producing light, rhythmic letterforms with a casual, spontaneous quality typical of informal script typefaces.
summary: A pointed-brush handwritten script with 936 characters, 42 ligatures and 12 decorative ornaments, aimed at casual and playful design contexts.
description: Drawn with a pointed brush tool, Zabawa produces light, rhythmic letterforms that suggest spontaneous doodling rather than formal calligraphy. The single font ships with extensive OpenType support: alternate characters for both cases, 42 ligatures, lining and oldstyle figures, fractions and a set of 12 ornaments. The large character set makes it a practical choice for packaging, greeting cards and social content where an informal hand-drawn tone is needed.
foundry_blurb: The Northern Block describe Zabawa as a playful handwritten typeface that explores the pointed brush tool, capturing a hand-drawn personality through light, rhythmic forms. They liken its energy to spontaneous doodles on a napkin or scribbles on a shopping list, and position it as a go-to for injecting fun into projects, backed by 936 characters and a full suite of OpenType features.

name: Planer
classification_notes: Planer is a geometric sans with rounded terminals, drawing explicitly from VAG Rounded and infusing that tradition with contemporary geometric construction. The rounded corners and warm geometry place it firmly in the rounded geometric sans subcategory.
summary: A rounded geometric sans inspired by woodworking craft and VAG Rounded, offering 16 fonts across eight weights with italic companions.
description: Drawing from the lineage of VAG Rounded, Planer adds contemporary geometry to produce a typeface that sits between warm and precise. Version 2.0 introduced an additional bold weight for headline work, improved optical compensation for small text, and ten alternative characters alongside OpenType features including circled numbers, case-sensitive forms and arrows. At 16 fonts, the family has enough range for identity and editorial use where a softer geometric voice is needed.
foundry_blurb: The Northern Block describe Planer as inspired by the honesty and skill of handcrafted woodworking, blending tradition with modernity. They draw a direct line to classic rounded typefaces like VAG Rounded, while adding contemporary geometry to achieve a balance of warmth and precision. The name references the woodworking plane, reflecting the attention to detail that produces smooth surfaces, echoed in the typeface's rounded corners.

name: Metral
classification_notes: Explicitly described as geometric sans-serif, built on a grid structure with smooth corners softening the geometry into a warmer, more approachable form.
summary: A geometric sans built on a grid structure with softened corners, available in seven weights with obliques.
description: Constructed on a strict grid yet tempered by smooth corners, Metral sits between cool precision and everyday approachability. The family runs to seven weights, each with an oblique, and includes over 650 characters per style along with OpenType features covering alternate letterforms, fractions and tabular figures. Language support extends across Western, Southern and Central European Latin scripts, and version 2.0 brought improved OpenType implementation throughout.
foundry_blurb: The Northern Block describe Metral as a geometric sans-serif that balances precision with approachability, using clean grid-based lines softened by smooth corners. They position it for contemporary branding and editorial layouts, noting its sharp interplay between structure and subtle detailing. The complete family comprises 14 fonts across seven weights with obliques, over 650 characters per style, and broad European language support, remastered to version 2.0 for enhanced OpenType features.

name: Cagney
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a modern grotesque slab with Egyptian-style origins from the 19th century, combining heavy and light stroke contrast with ball terminals typical of the grotesque slab genre.
summary: A seven-weight grotesque slab drawn from 19th-century Egyptian types, with ball terminals, ink traps and humanist stroke contrast giving it an animated quality.
description: Built across seven weights with matching italics, Cagney takes the Egyptian slab tradition as its starting point and applies deep cuts and ink traps alongside outsized ball terminals and off-axis angles. The result sits between historical slab warmth and modern-day legibility, with over 900 characters per style covering Western, Southern and Central European Latin. Fourteen number variants and a full set of OpenType alternates make it well-equipped for editorial and identity work.
foundry_blurb: The Northern Block describe Cagney as a modern grotesque slab with humanist touches, inspired by 19th-century Egyptian type. Outsized ball terminals, off-track angles and contrasting heavy and light strokes combine with deep cuts and ink traps to create an impactful typeface that mixes historical imperfection with contemporary utility.

name: Dekal
classification_notes: A digital revival of Blackline, Dekal uses contrasting thick and thin parallel lines to create a distinctive inline display character rooted in the dry transfer lettering era of the late 1960s and early 1970s.
summary: A digital revival of Wolf Magin's 1969 Blackline typeface, rebuilt with three interchangeable inline line styles for screen and print use.
description: Originally drawn by Wolf Magin in 1969, Blackline's parallel thick-and-thin inline structure gave it a distinctive presence in the dry transfer lettering era. This revival expands the line spacing and character construction for improved screen rendering, while retaining the period character. Three interchangeable line styles, thirteen stylistic sets and over 500 characters per style make it well suited to apparel, posters and editorial display.
foundry_blurb: The Northern Block describe Dekal as a revival of Blackline, originally designed by Wolf Magin in 1969. The contrast between thick and thin parallel lines creates a visual identity familiar from the dry transfer era of the late 1960s and early 1970s. The digital version features more expansive lines and spacing to aid lowercase construction and screen rendering, with OpenType features including lining and tabular figures, fractions, standard ligatures, and thirteen stylistic sets, covering Western, Southern, and Central European languages.

name: Maggy
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a display typeface, originating from the 36 Days of Type challenge, with experimental and unconventional letterform design as its defining character.
summary: A single-weight experimental display typeface grown from a 36 Days of Type challenge, sold in aid of Maggie's cancer charity.
description: Developed during the annual 36 Days of Type event, Maggy is an experimental display face with a deliberately unconventional character. The single font contains over 310 glyphs with six stylistic sets, proportional and tabular figures, and Latin coverage across Western, Southern and Central Europe. All proceeds go to Maggie's cancer charity, making the purchase itself part of the typeface's stated purpose.
foundry_blurb: The Northern Block describe Maggy as a charity typeface and a tribute to the unconventional, donating all proceeds to Maggie's cancer charity. Born from the 36 Days of Type challenge, it includes one display style, over 310 glyphs, six stylistic sets and broad European Latin language support.

name: Modum
classification_notes: A low-contrast serif with smooth radius details and carefully drawn angles, positioning it between transitional and contemporary serif classifications, optimised for digital and editorial applications.
summary: A low-contrast contemporary serif in eight weights with true italics, awarded a Merit at Hiii Typography 2015 and designed for editorial and text-heavy digital work.
description: With over 900 characters per style and an extensive OpenType feature set, Modum covers twelve numeral variations, small caps, and alternates for a, e, g and y. Its low-contrast letterforms balance smooth radius details with considered angles, making it well suited to newspapers, editorials and hierarchical book layouts. Version 2.0 brought refined weight distribution and expanded typographic flexibility across all sixteen styles.
foundry_blurb: The Northern Block describe Modum as a contemporary serif that merges traditional influences with modern design precision, citing its low-contrast structure and balance of smooth radius details and drawn angles as central to its crisp aesthetic. They position it as an ideal choice for editorials, newspapers and text-based books, noting its Merit Award at the Hiii International Typography Design competition in 2015 and the improvements introduced in version 2.0, including refined weight distribution and advanced OpenType features.

name: Hubsch
classification_notes: Classified as an experimental sans-serif, drawing on chisel-tipped marker pen geometry where angular strokes meet flowing curves, producing a distinctly brutalist character rather than falling into any conventional grotesque or geometric tradition.
summary: A chisel-tipped marker pen inspires this seven-weight experimental sans, where sharp angles and flowing curves produce a brutalist character suited to sports venues, video games and large-scale signage.
description: Drawn on the principles of a chisel-tipped marker pen, Hubsch balances thick and thin strokes through abrupt angular transitions rather than conventional modulation. The family ships as fourteen fonts plus a variable font, covering seven weights each with an oblique, and over 600 characters per style. OpenType support runs to ten number variations, ten stylistic sets and broad coverage across Western, Southern and Central European Latin.
foundry_blurb: The Northern Block describe Hubsch as an experimental sans-serif following the drawing principles of a chisel-tipped marker pen, where sharp angles meet flowing curves in a balance of thick and thin strokes. They position it as a distinctive, brutalist typeface suited to video games, mobile applications, architecture, sports venues and arenas, with seven weights, obliques, over 600 characters per style and extensive OpenType features including ten number variations and ten stylistic sets.

name: Angusta
classification_notes: High-contrast, extra-condensed letterforms with sharp elongated serifs draw directly from 19th-century American wood type traditions, placing this firmly in the display serif category with a condensed display subcategory.
summary: An extra-condensed, high-contrast display serif inspired by 19th-century American wood type, awarded at the Communication Arts Typography Competition in 2023.
description: Six weights with matching obliques and over 400 characters per style, built around tightly spaced letterforms with sharp elongated serifs that add a sense of forward movement. The extreme contrast between thick and thin strokes gives it the visual punch required for headlines and large-scale typography, while the refined serif detail prevents it reading as merely decorative. Variable font support and broad Western, Southern and Central European language coverage make it practical across editorial and branding work.
foundry_blurb: The Northern Block describe Angusta as a highly versatile and elegant display serif drawing inspiration from American wood type of the 19th century. They highlight its extra-condensed design, tightly spaced letterforms and high contrast between thick and thin strokes as giving it a bold, striking appearance suited to headlines and large-scale typography. Six weights with matching obliques include over 400 characters per style, with OpenType features covering inferiors, superiors, fractions, tabular and lining figures, and language support for Western, Southern and Central Europe.

name: Monsal Gothic
classification_notes: Described by the foundry as inspired by American Gothic typefaces, with clean compact proportions and strong readability characteristics typical of the grotesque tradition.
summary: A seven-weight grotesque sans drawing on American Gothic typefaces, designed for text-heavy print and digital publishing.
description: Rooted in American Gothic traditions, Monsal Gothic offers clean compact proportions across seven weights with matching italics and a variable font. Over 600 characters cover Western, Southern and Central European Latin with OpenType features including six numeral variants, small caps, case-sensitive forms and ligatures. The family suits text-centric work in books, magazines and digital interfaces where readability at running text sizes is the priority.
foundry_blurb: The Northern Block describe Monsal Gothic as a sans-serif fusing contemporary design simplicity with enduring typographic elegance, drawing inspiration from iconic American Gothic typefaces renowned for readability and versatility. The family includes seven weights and italics, over 600 characters with an alternative lowercase g, and OpenType features covering six numeral styles, small caps, case-sensitive forms and ligatures, with language support for Western, Southern and Central Europe.

name: Hackman
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a humanist sans-serif with subtle squarish geometry and classical proportions, placing it firmly in the humanist sans subcategory.
summary: A humanist sans with squarish geometry and classical proportions, offered in nine weights with italics and a variable font.
description: Combining humanist letter construction with subtle squarish geometry, this typeface achieves a clean, linear quality across nine weights from Thin to Black with matching italics. The design was developed across six masters to maintain rhythmic balance through the weight range, and version 2.0 brought improved OpenType features and better screen performance. Over 600 characters cover Western, Southern and Central European languages, with six numeral variants and alternate lowercase forms for a and e.
foundry_blurb: The Northern Block describe Hackman as a contemporary humanist sans-serif with subtle squarish geometry, combining distinctive letter shapes with classical proportions to produce a clean, linear typeface with dynamic character. The family includes nine weights and italics, variable font support, and over 600 characters, remastered to version 2.0 for improved OpenType features and screen usability.

name: Byker
classification_notes: Explicitly described as a geometric sans-serif, constructed from a technical grid with handmade curves overlaid, giving it a hybrid geometric-organic character.
summary: A geometric sans built on a digital grid but softened with handmade curves, available in eight weights with italics and a variable font.
description: Constructed from a technical grid and finished with handmade curves, Byker sits between the precision of a geometric sans and something warmer and more organic. Version 2.0 adds an ultra display weight, a variable font, and expanded OpenType features covering eleven number variations, small caps and alternate lowercase characters. Language support spans Western, Southern and Central European Latin, with over 900 characters per style.
foundry_blurb: The Northern Block describe Byker as a geometric sans-serif that blends technology with handcrafted forms, built digitally from a technical grid and overlaid with handmade curves to create a font that is precise yet organic, with subtle movement and personality without being too clinical. Remastered to version 2.0, the family now includes an ultra display weight, improved OpenType features and a variable font.

name: Facto
classification_notes: Facto is a geometric sans with unembellished, slightly condensed letterforms drawing directly from industrial typeface traditions; its clean construction and circular forms place it firmly in the geometric grotesque camp.
summary: A geometric sans with a slight condensed quality and industrial roots, available in six weights with obliques and variable font support.
description: Remastered to version 2.0, this geometric sans draws from industrial typeface traditions with clean, unembellished letterforms and slightly condensed curves. The family covers six weights with matching obliques, over 700 characters, and a comprehensive set of OpenType features including eight numeral variants, case-sensitive forms and ligatures. Its crisp legibility makes it a practical choice for UI design, web and mobile applications.
foundry_blurb: The Northern Block describe Facto as a sleek, modern typeface inspired by industrial-style design, built around a clean geometric ethos. Version 2.0 refines its industrial aesthetic and adds variable font support, enhanced OpenType features and improved digital usability, making it suited to interfaces, mobile and web contexts where legibility is a priority.

name: Leida
classification_notes: Leida is described as a refined serif type family designed for text reading, with a cheerful personality and balanced functionality, placing it within contemporary text serifs rather than any strictly historical classification.
summary: A contemporary text serif in eight weights with true italics, broad OpenType feature support and coverage across Western, Southern and Central European Latin.
description: Leida is a refined text serif designed for everyday reading scenarios, drawing on typographic history while being drafted directly in modern design software to maintain clean line quality. Each of the eight weights ships with a true italic and over 900 characters, with OpenType features covering fourteen number variations including oldstyle, tabular, fractions, inferiors and circled figures. Alternate forms for lowercase a, e, g and y, plus small caps, give the family flexibility for editorial and book work.
foundry_blurb: The Northern Block describe Leida as a well-refined serif type family that balances functionality with a cheerful personality, drawing on typographic experience, history and current technology to produce a typeface suited to everyday text reading. Letterforms were drafted directly within the application to benefit from the latest tools and improve line quality. The complete family includes eight weights with true italics, over 900 characters per style, fourteen OpenType number variations and language support for Western, Southern and Central Europe.

name: Moret
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a serif display type family inspired by 20th-century European sign painting, blending calligraphic concepts; the display category is confirmed by its headline and editorial focus and emphatic character.
summary: A display serif family drawing on 20th-century European sign painting, available in five weights with upright and oblique styles.
description: Combining calligraphic influences with the emphatic weight contrast of sign-painted letterforms, Moret is designed primarily for headlines and editorials. The family runs across five weights from Light to Black, each with an oblique companion, and includes a variable font alongside the ten static styles. Each font covers 466 glyphs with support for over 265 Latin-based languages, and OpenType features include ligatures, fractions and tabular figures.
foundry_blurb: The Northern Block describe Moret as a serif display type family inspired by 20th-century European sign painting, blending several calligraphic concepts to produce a dynamic and emphatic typeface suited to editorials and headlines. It is available in five weights and two styles — upright and oblique — with a variable font included in the complete family.

name: Fagun
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a humanist sans-serif with calligraphic roots, combining broad nib pen-style strokes with straight-line geometry and diagonal contrast typical of humanist sans classification.
summary: A humanist sans-serif combining broad nib pen strokes with straight-line geometry, awarded a Certificate of Typographic Excellence at TDC69.
description: Fagun blends calligraphic pen-drawing traditions with geometric construction, producing diagonal contrast that sets up a productive tension between internal and external letterform lines. Seven weights run from Thin to Black, each with matching italics, and the family ships with over 700 characters per style covering Western, Southern and Central European Latin. OpenType features include nine number variants, small caps and alternates for a, e, g, l and y, making it well equipped for demanding text-setting work.
foundry_blurb: The Northern Block describe Fagun as a humanist sans-serif with calligraphic roots, combining broad nib pen-style strokes with straight-line geometry for long-form text settings. The family spans seven weights with matching italics, over 700 characters per style, extensive OpenType features including nine number variations, small caps and alternates, and language support for Western, Southern and Central Europe. In 2023 it received the Certificate of Typographic Excellence at TDC69.

name: Tondu
classification_notes: Tondu is a heavyweight display typeface inspired by early 1900s film poster lettering, with strong upright forms and smooth curved details that place it firmly in the display category with historical serif influences.
summary: A single-weight display typeface drawn from early 20th-century film poster lettering, with upright forms and smooth curves suited to bold print applications.
description: Inspired by film posters of the early 1900s, Tondu pairs strong upright letterforms with smooth curved details to produce a heavyweight display face that works at scale. Version 2.0, released in 2021, brought the technical standards up to date with over 490 characters including lining figures, fractions and coverage for Western, Southern and Central European languages. Well suited to apparel, poster and billboard work where a period-inflected boldness is needed.
foundry_blurb: The Northern Block describe Tondu as a straightforward display typeface inspired by early 1900s film posters, combining strong upright forms with smooth curved details for use on apparel, billboards, books and posters. The 2021 version 2.0 remaster brings the font up to modern technical standards, offering over 490 characters with OpenType features including lining figures, fractions and broad European language support.

name: Halcom
classification_notes: Explicitly geometric in construction, drawn from the tradition of 1920s geometric sans-serifs with Futura and Avenir as primary references. The design balances pure geometric forms with optical correction.
summary: A geometric sans rooted in the Futura tradition, balancing pure mathematical construction with optical correction across 16 styles and a variable font.
description: Drawing from 1920s geometric sans-serifs, Halcom takes Futura and its descendants as a starting point rather than a template, adjusting each character for optical correctness rather than strict geometry. The family ships with 550 characters per style, alternate letterforms for the lowercase a, g and y, five numeral sets and manually edited kerning. It earned a Silver Award at the Hiii Brand International Typography Competition in 2015.
foundry_blurb: The Northern Block describe Halcom as an award-winning geometric sans inspired by the historic geometrics of the 1920s, particularly Futura. They position it not as pastiche but as a close investigation of how Futura informed later designs such as Avenir, with each character carefully adjusted to sit between pure mathematics and optical correctness.

name: Waldo
classification_notes: A heavyweight stencil display face with rectangular slot cuts, explicitly described as stencil-focused and display-oriented, designed for posters, film titles and apparel rather than text use.
summary: A heavyweight stencil display face drawn from a 1973 Planet of the Apes film poster, offered in four styles including Stencil, Outline and Shadow.
description: Rectangular slots cut through dense, narrow forms give Waldo its stencil character, with the design tracing back to the poster artwork for The Battle For The Planet of the Apes (1973). The family ships in four styles — Black, Stencil, Outline and Shadow — across a single weight, with over 400 characters and OpenType support for fractions, tabular figures and a handful of alternates. Western, Southern and Central European Latin coverage makes it workable across most European markets.
foundry_blurb: The Northern Block describe Waldo as a bold, stencil-focused display typeface loosely based on a 1973 science fiction movie poster for The Battle For The Planet of the Apes. Narrow rectangular slots cut into heavyweight forms create a stylish and energetic font suited to apparel, books, film titles, packaging and posters. The family includes four unique styles — Black, Stencil, Outline and Shadow — with over 400 characters and OpenType features covering alternate glyphs, figures and fractions.

name: Kopik
classification_notes: Described as a hand-drawn script inspired by 1960s architectural drafting handwriting, drawn with a Copic marker pen and traced digitally; monolinear and casual rather than formal calligraphic script.
summary: A monolinear hand-drawn script rooted in 1960s architectural drafting handwriting, originated with a Copic marker pen and refined digitally.
description: Drawn with a Copic 1.0 Multiliner pen and traced digitally via stylus and tablet, Kopik retains the casual rhythm of draftsman lettering whilst smoothing out imperfections. Three weights run from Light to Bold with matching italics, each containing 440 characters including alternate forms for a, g and y, plus four numeral variants. OpenType features cover inferiors, superiors and fractions, with language support across Western, Southern and Central European Latin.
foundry_blurb: The Northern Block describe Kopik as a modern hand-drawn script inspired by 1960s architectural handwriting, originated with a Copic 1.0 Multiliner pen and traced digitally to produce a simple, readable, monolinear typeface with a marker pen quality suitable for a wide range of written applications.

name: Paradroid Mono Soft
classification_notes: A monospaced typeface sitting between grotesque and geometric styles, with rounded terminals that add softness to an otherwise neutral, precision-driven design.
summary: A rounded variant of Paradroid Mono that softens its geometric grotesque origins with curved terminals while keeping a neutral, functional character.
description: Sitting midway between grotesque and geometric styles, this monospaced family adds rounded edges to the balanced proportions of the original Paradroid Mono. Four weights with matching italics and over 450 characters per style make it practical across code, UI and editorial contexts where some warmth is wanted without sacrificing clarity. OpenType features include stylistic zeros, fractions, numerators and denominators, with coverage across Western, Southern and Central European Latin.
foundry_blurb: The Northern Block describe Paradroid Mono Soft as a refined, rounded adaptation of the original Paradroid monospaced font, sitting at the midpoint of the grotesque and geometric style spectrum. The rounded edges introduce a subtle softness while maintaining neutral and modern aesthetics, combining humanist legibility with geometric precision for applications where clarity, readability and a touch of warmth are desired.

name: Kylo Sans
classification_notes: Described by the foundry as a blend of humanist, grotesque and geometric influences; the humanist-grotesque hybrid subcategory best captures its primary character, with geometric elements playing a minor role.
summary: A twelve-font sans-serif blending humanist warmth, grotesque structure and a touch of geometry, with six weights and matching italics.
description: Kylo Sans draws from three stylistic traditions, humanist, grotesque and geometric, to produce a typeface with restrained personality suited to extended text. Six weights run from Thin to Black with matching italics, and over 700 characters cover Western, Southern and Central European languages. Version 2.0 brought improved OpenType features including six numeral variants, small caps, ligatures and alternates for Q, a, g, l and y.
foundry_blurb: The Northern Block describe Kylo Sans as a carefully blended typeface combining humanist, grotesque and geometric influences into a readable design with an exact and understated personality. The design process involved hand-eye adjustment across three master weights to extend usability across text layouts, and the family was remastered to version 2.0 for improved OpenType features.

name: Mekon
classification_notes: A heavyweight display type digitised from Peter Steiner's Black Body (1973), featuring Pac-Man-inspired circular shapes and keyhole counters that place it firmly in the display category with a retro-geometric character.
summary: A remastered heavyweight display face derived from Peter Steiner's 1973 Black Body, built around circular Pac-Man forms and tight keyhole counters.
description: Digitised and expanded from Peter Steiner's Black Body of 1973, Mekon is a bold display type with a retro-geometric personality driven by circular counters and Pac-Man-like curves. Version 2.0 (2021) brings the typeface up to current technical standards, with over 460 characters across four styles including a gradient option. Suited to posters, book covers and apparel where a punchy, retro-flavoured weight is needed.
foundry_blurb: The Northern Block describe Mekon as a modern heavyweight typeface digitised and expanded from Peter Steiner's Black Body (1973), combining retro Pac-Man shapes with small keyhole counters for a bold and witty result. Version 2.0 (2021) is a remastered edition meeting contemporary technical standards, with over 460 characters, four unique styles, a free gradient option, and OpenType features including digital numerals, lining figures, fractions and alternates, covering Western, Southern and Central European languages.

name: Tengu
classification_notes: A wireframe multiline display face digitised from a 1973 original; the intricate parallel-line structure places it firmly in decorative display territory with no body-text application.
summary: A single-weight multiline display face digitised from Gustavo Pardo Sarmiento's 1973 Tangui, built on an intricate wire-like parallel-line structure.
description: Digitised and expanded from Gustavo Pardo Sarmiento's Tangui (1973), the typeface uses a wireframe construction of fine parallel lines to produce an effect that reads as simultaneously retro and futuristic. Over 400 characters are included with OpenType support for lining figures, fractions and Western, Southern and Central European languages. Best suited to display contexts such as posters, apparel and cover work where the ornate structure can be read at scale.
foundry_blurb: The Northern Block describe Tengu as a multiline display typeface digitised and expanded from Gustavo Pardo Sarmiento's Tangui (1973), noting that its intricate wirelike framework creates an elegant yet futuristic font suited to apparel, books, t-shirts and posters. The font contains over 400 characters with OpenType features including digital numerals, lining figures, fractions and language support for Western, Southern and Central Europe.

name: Vitro
classification_notes: Described by the foundry as a rectangular sans serif with grotesque influence; the geometric, squared construction with optical corrections places it in the grotesque-adjacent geometric sans category.
summary: A rectangular grotesque-inflected sans with nine weights and matching italics, built for branding and display use.
description: Vitro combines a squared, technical structure with grotesque optical adjustments to produce a clean, contemporary sans. Nine weights run from Thin to Black with italic companions, and each style carries over 450 characters with OpenType features including fractions, tabular figures and numerators. Language support covers Western, Southern and Central Europe, and the family was remastered to version 2.0 for improved OpenType usability.
foundry_blurb: The Northern Block describe Vitro as a rectangular sans serif with a pinch of grotesque, noting that its solid technical appearance was achieved through careful optical adjustment. The family is positioned for branding purposes including brochures, logos, packaging, posters, signage and websites, and was remastered to version 2.0 for enhanced OpenType features and usability.

name: Woolworth
classification_notes: Explicitly described as a contemporary grotesque inspired by late 19th-century grotesque designs, with handcrafted warmth typical of the humanised grotesque subcategory.
summary: A contemporary grotesque in six weights with italics, drawing on late 19th-century designs for a clean, optically balanced text face.
description: Woolworth takes cues from the grotesques of the late 19th century, refined into a 12-font family spanning six weights with matching italics. Alternate forms for lowercase a, e and g, along with seven numeral variants, small caps and ligatures, give it enough flexibility for considered typesetting. Language support covers Western, Southern and Central European Latin, and version 2.0 was released in 2022.
foundry_blurb: The Northern Block describe Woolworth as a modern sans-serif inspired by late 19th-century grotesque designs, developed with attention to balance and purity of form. The family includes six weights and italics, over 600 characters, OpenType features such as small caps, ligatures and seven numeral variants, and language support across Western, Southern and Central Europe.

name: Duran
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a geometric sans with industrial quality, inspired by technical style letterforms and simple construction, placing it firmly in the geometric sans subcategory.
summary: A compact geometric sans with seven weights and matching italics, built for both large-format display and body text across modern layouts.
description: Drawn with simple construction and a technical character, Duran sits in the tradition of industrial geometric sans designs. Its laterally compact proportions make it space-efficient across editorial and branding layouts without sacrificing legibility at text sizes. The full family of 14 fonts includes over 670 characters per style and a thorough OpenType feature set covering eight numeral variants, stylistic alternates, arrows, game symbols and Western, Southern and Central European language support.
foundry_blurb: The Northern Block describe Duran as a strong, versatile geometric sans with industrial quality, inspired by technical style letterforms with simple construction. They highlight its compact lateral shape as space-saving across layouts and position it as useful in both large format and body text across a wide range of modern applications.

name: Syke Mono
classification_notes: A purpose-built monospaced companion to the Syke sans-serif family, with fixed-width spacing and features tailored for coding and screen interfaces.
summary: A monospaced companion to the Syke family, designed for coding and screen interfaces across five weights with true italics.
description: Derived from the proportional Syke typeface, Syke Mono adapts its forms into fixed-width spacing with careful attention to characters that affect alignment and readability, including alternative versions of lowercase a, i, l and r. Each of the ten styles covers over 590 characters, with five variations of numerals, manually edited kerning and OpenType features. Initially released in 2016 and updated in 2021, it suits coding environments, database applications and ebook platforms.
foundry_blurb: The Northern Block describe Syke Mono as a monospaced companion to their Syke type family, noting that details are carefully drawn into selected characters to improve centre alignment, function and readability. Available in five weights with true italics and over 590 characters per style, they position it as ideal for computer coding, database applications, ebooks and other screen-based interfaces.

name: Knul
classification_notes: Knul presents a mono-line geometric construction with engineered precision balanced by subtle humanist touches, placing it firmly in the geometric sans tradition.
summary: A mono-line geometric sans in six weights with italics, balancing engineered precision with subtle hand-drawn touches for identity and editorial use.
description: Knul pairs a clean mono-line structure with careful geometric proportions, softened by delicate manual refinements that prevent it feeling purely mechanical. Six weights run from Thin to Black, each with a matching italic, and each style carries over 600 characters including six numeral variants covering inferiors, superiors, fractions, lining and tabular figures. Language support extends across Western, Southern and Central European Latin scripts as well as Cyrillic, with the family remastered to version 2.0 for improved OpenType behaviour.
foundry_blurb: The Northern Block describe Knul as an elegant modern typeface with a subtle mono-line appearance, noting that balanced engineered geometry combined with delicate hand touches allows for practical typesetting without complications. They position it as best suited to identity, editorial, advertising and software applications, and note its use in FIFA Football by EA Sports between 2012 and 2016.

name: Loew
classification_notes: Explicitly described as geometric-based, with pure mechanical shapes drawn from early industrial design traditions, placing it firmly in the geometric sans-serif subcategory.
summary: A geometric sans-serif with industrial roots, adjusted with subtle humanist corrections for readability across both display and text sizes.
description: Built from mechanical forms influenced by early industrial designers, Loew tempers its strict geometry with careful optical adjustments to keep characters legible at small sizes. Each style carries over 800 characters, with alternate lowercase a, e and g, seven numeral variants, small caps with accents and manually edited kerning. A practical, well-equipped family suited to branding, editorial and interface work where clean geometry is wanted without sacrificing usability.
foundry_blurb: The Northern Block describe Loew as a geometric-based sans-serif influenced by early industrial designers, where pure mechanical shapes are carefully adjusted for the right form, function and usability. Subtle human touches combined with technical detail provide great readability at both large and small sizes, with an extensive character set including alternate letterforms, multiple numeral styles, small caps and OpenType features across 16 fonts.

name: Loew Next
classification_notes: Loew Next is a clean, upright geometric sans-serif with alternate single-storey letterforms and a neutral, functional character consistent with the geometric tradition.
summary: A reworked geometric sans with eight weights and broad script coverage across Latin, Cyrillic and Greek for international use.
description: An update to the original Loew (2014), refined for greater consistency across weights and expanded to cover Cyrillic, Greek and Extended Latin. Eight weights run from Thin to Ultra, each with a matching italic, and OpenType features include alternate lowercase forms for a, e, g and l, seven numeral variations and small caps with accents. Suited to pan-European branding and corporate communication where a clean, upright geometric is needed at scale.
foundry_blurb: The Northern Block describe Loew Next as a modern, honest and straightforward sans-serif for global communication, reworked from the original Loew released in 2014. The update refines consistency across weights and expands non-Latin script support, with Cyrillic, Greek and Extended Latin coverage alongside extensive OpenType features including alternate lowercase forms, seven numeral variations and small caps.

name: Loew Next Arabic
classification_notes: Loew Next Arabic extends the geometric sans design of Loew into Arabic script, maintaining the clean, modern proportions of the Latin family while respecting Arabic calligraphic tradition.
summary: A geometric sans family that extends Loew Next into Arabic script, designed to pair with its Latin counterpart across bilingual settings.
description: Loew Next Arabic adapts the clean geometry of the Loew family for Arabic script, with careful attention to Arabic proportions, rhythm and calligraphic tradition. The family ships in eight fonts and supports advanced OpenType features including localised forms and initial, medial and final forms, as well as alternate lowercase forms and small caps with accents for the Latin side. Intended for design work that must remain visually consistent across Arabic and Latin text.
foundry_blurb: The Northern Block describe Loew Next Arabic as bringing the clarity and modernity of Loew into the Arabic script while maintaining compatibility with Loew Next. They note the design was reworked with close attention to Arabic proportions, rhythm and calligraphic tradition, and that the family supports Arabic and Extended Latin with advanced OpenType features including localised, initial, medial and final forms.

name: Loew Variable
classification_notes: A geometric sans-serif with circular, mechanical forms drawn from early industrial design, with humanist corrections applied to improve legibility at small sizes.
summary: A geometric sans-serif variable font with condensed and extended width axes, rooted in early industrial design with subtle humanist corrections for readability.
description: Built from pure mechanical shapes with careful optical adjustments, Loew Variable covers the full weight range of the static Loew family in a single variable font file. Width variants from condensed to extended are included, making it workable across narrow text columns, body copy and large-scale print. The industrial influence keeps the forms disciplined without tipping into coldness.
foundry_blurb: The Northern Block describe Loew Variable as a geometric sans-serif influenced by early industrial designers, with mechanical shapes adjusted for correct form, function and usability. Released as a variable font in 2022, it encompasses all Loew weights plus intermediate styles, and adds condensed and extended width axes for use in tight spaces and large-format applications.

name: Nauman Neue
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a humanist sans-serif with broad, open letterforms and precise geometry, optimised for screen legibility across multiple weights and widths.
summary: A screen-optimised humanist sans in ten weights and three widths, built for legibility at small sizes across digital interfaces.
description: Built with broad, open letterforms and precise geometry, this family targets web and interface design where clarity at small sizes matters most. Sixty styles span ten weights with matching italics across condensed, semi-condensed and regular widths, giving designers considerable latitude for layout flexibility. OpenType features include seven numeral variants, small caps, alternates for a, e, I and M, and language support covering Western, Southern and Central European scripts plus Vietnamese.
foundry_blurb: The Northern Block describe Nauman Neue as a modern humanist sans-serif designed specifically for screen use, with broad open letterforms crafted with precise geometry for functional legibility. Expanded in 2021 to sixty styles, it covers ten weights, three widths and matching italics, with 900 characters per style and OpenType features including numeral variations, small caps, alternates and extensive Latin language support.

name: Paradroid
classification_notes: Positioned explicitly between grotesque and geometric traditions, Paradroid blends the humanist legibility of grotesque typefaces with the structured, clean forms of geometric design.
summary: A sans-serif family that blends grotesque and geometric influences, offered in seven proportional weights and four monospaced weights, each with italics.
description: Sitting between the warmth of a grotesque and the precision of a geometric, Paradroid aims for neutral legibility across a broad range of applications. The family ships with 750 characters per style, alternate lowercase 'a' and 'g', and twelve numeral variations including stylistic zeros. OpenType features cover inferiors, superiors, fractions, case-sensitive punctuation and language support for Western, Southern, Central European and Vietnamese scripts.
foundry_blurb: The Northern Block describe Paradroid as a versatile sans-serif positioned at the intersection of grotesque and geometric typographic traditions, combining humanist legibility with clean, structured forms. The complete family includes seven proportionally spaced weights and four monospaced weights, all with matching italics, 750 characters, alternative lowercase letters, twelve variations of numerals, and broad European plus Vietnamese language support.

name: Nurom
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a contemporary Grotesk influenced by early grotesque style, prioritising neutrality and legibility while avoiding direct historical revival.
summary: A contemporary grotesque in six weights designed to offer clarity and strength across a broad range of applications without leaning on historical revival.
description: Nurom draws on the early grotesque tradition, aiming for neutrality and legibility with enough personality to stand apart in a crowded market. Each of the six weights contains over 400 characters with OpenType features including decimal figures, fractions and case-sensitive punctuation. Language support covers Western, Southern and Central European Latin character sets.
foundry_blurb: The Northern Block describe Nurom as a contemporary sans-serif influenced by early grotesque style, neutral and legible in purpose but with a fresh personality. The aim was not historic revival but to create a new Grotesk offering strength, clarity and function across a wide range of applications, with six weights and comprehensive OpenType feature support.

name: Corbert Compact
classification_notes: Corbert Compact is a geometric sans-serif, derived from the wider Corbert family, distinguished by its extreme narrowness and space efficiency while retaining geometric construction principles.
summary: An extremely narrow geometric sans, narrower than Corbert Condensed, offered in nine weights with matching italics for space-constrained layouts.
description: Derived from the Corbert geometric sans family, this compact variant pushes narrowness further than the condensed cut, covering nine weights from Thin to Black each with a matching italic. Over 540 characters per style include five numeral variants, fractions and alternate letterforms for a, e and g, with Latin coverage extending to Western, Southern and Central European languages. Best suited to tight editorial columns, signage and any setting where horizontal space is at a premium.
foundry_blurb: The Northern Block describe Corbert Compact as an exceptionally space-efficient variant of the Corbert family, surpassing even the condensed version in narrowness. They position it as a solution for extreme space optimisation across tight text layouts, editorial design and space-limited projects, while retaining the geometric sans-serif qualities of the original Corbert.

name: Corbert Condensed
classification_notes: The typeface is explicitly described as geometric sans-serif, sharing the precision and clarity characteristics of the parent Corbert family, drawn with clean geometric forms across nine weights.
summary: A condensed geometric sans derived from the Corbert family, running approximately 15% narrower than its parent to suit tight text layouts and narrow columns.
description: Nine weights with matching italics give the family a broad range for editorial and publication work where space is constrained. Each style ships with 540 characters, five numeral variants, alternative lowercase forms for a, e and g, and manually edited kerning. The condensed cut pairs with the original Corbert for a unified typographic system across varied layout conditions.
foundry_blurb: The Northern Block describe Corbert Condensed as a space-efficient, narrower variant of the original Corbert, approximately 15% slimmer and designed to optimise space in text layouts and complex design projects. They highlight its geometric sans-serif characteristics and position the pairing of Corbert and Corbert Condensed as a seamless, unified typographic solution for a wide range of applications.

name: Corbert
classification_notes: Explicitly Bauhaus-influenced geometric sans-serif with optically adjusted circular forms and a minimalist, functional character consistent with the geometric sans tradition.
summary: A Bauhaus-influenced geometric sans-serif with optically adjusted letterforms, available across nine weights with matching italics.
description: First released in 2013 and updated in 2020, Corbert draws on early modernist and Bauhaus principles to produce a clean, functional geometric sans. Nine weights from Thin to Black each carry a matching italic, and over 540 characters per style cover Western, Southern and Central European languages. OpenType features include five numeral variants, fractions, and alternate forms of the lowercase a, e and g.
foundry_blurb: The Northern Block describe Corbert as a geometric sans-serif influenced by Bauhaus and early modernist principles, featuring optically adjusted shapes for natural legibility and a minimalist, functional character. Originally released in 2013 and updated in 2020 to reflect user feedback and technological advances, it ships with nine weights, matching italics, over 540 characters per style, and OpenType features including multiple numeral variants and language support for Western, Southern and Central Europe.

name: Corbert Wide
classification_notes: Corbert Wide is explicitly described as a geometric sans-serif, extended as a wide variant of the Corbert family for display use. Its broad character width and geometric construction place it firmly in the geometric sans subcategory.
summary: A wide geometric sans cut from the Corbert family, built across nine weights with matching italics for headline and display work.
description: An expanded variant of The Northern Block's Corbert, broadened specifically for display-scale use such as headlines, banners and branding. The family runs nine weights from Thin to Black, each with a matching italic, and each style covers over 540 characters with OpenType features including five numeral variants, fractions and alternates for lowercase a, e and g. Language support spans Western, Southern and Central European Latin.
foundry_blurb: The Northern Block describe Corbert Wide as an expansive and impactful extension of the original Corbert typeface, tailored for display purposes. It maintains Corbert's geometric sans-serif characteristics while introducing a broader character width for greater visual presence at larger formats. The family includes nine weights with matching italics, over 540 characters per style, and OpenType features covering multiple numeral styles, fractions and stylistic alternates.

name: Luke
classification_notes: Explicitly described as a contemporary adaptation of historic English Blackletter, inspired by 19th-century Caslon Foundry blackletter forms.
summary: A contemporary blackletter family drawn from 19th-century Caslon Foundry sources, spanning twelve styles from thin to thick contours with a variable font included.
