description: Named after the Caslon family tomb at St Luke Old Street in London, this family draws its core forms from a distinctive 19th-century Caslon blackletter. Twelve styles cover a range from light outlines to fully filled weights, making the family workable for both text-setting and display use. Each font includes 641 glyphs with OpenType support for ligatures, contextual alternates and fractions, covering 37 languages across South, East and Western Europe.
foundry_blurb: The Northern Block describe Luke as a contemporary adaptation of historic English Blackletter, inspired by a highly unique 19th-century Caslon Foundry typeface. The name pays tribute to the Caslon family tomb in the churchyard of St Luke Old Street, London. The family offers twelve styles ranging from thin to thick contours and half-filling to complete, suited to both text and display purposes, with 641 glyphs per font and broad OpenType feature support.

name: NuOrder
classification_notes: The foundry describes a balance of clean geometric lines with humanist elements and a natural stroke path mimicking handwriting fluidity, placing it firmly in the humanist sans-serif category with minimal stroke contrast.
summary: A nine-weight humanist sans with matching italics, drawing on natural stroke paths for approachable legibility across digital and print contexts.
description: NuOrder pairs geometric structure with humanist warmth through a natural stroke path that nods to handwriting fluidity. Nine weights from Thin to Black each carry a matching italic, giving the family 18 fonts in total, with over 550 characters per style and OpenType features covering five numeral variants, alternate lowercase a and g, and European language support. Remastered to version 2.0, it suits interface design, editorial work and brand communications where clean legibility and a degree of personality are both required.
foundry_blurb: The Northern Block describe NuOrder as a contemporary sans-serif that blends modern aesthetics with humanist elements, characterised by a balance of clean lines and subtle organic touch. It mimics handwriting fluidity through a natural stroke path, offering minimal stroke contrast for sleek legibility across digital and print applications. The family comprises nine weights with matching italics, over 550 characters per style, and wide European language support, updated to version 2.0 for enhanced OpenType features.

name: Blom
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a humanist sans with squarish character and reverse contrast; the geometric influence places it within the humanist sans category with a distinctive structural twist.
summary: A humanist sans with reverse contrast and squarish geometry, offering six weights with matching italics and broad Latin language support.
description: Blom pairs heavy horizontals with modern geometry to produce a reverse-contrast humanist sans that remains legible at small sizes. Six weights run from Thin to Black, each with a matching italic, and 463 characters per style cover Western, Southern and Central European languages. OpenType features include five numeral variants, ligatures, fractions, slashed zeros and case-sensitive forms.
foundry_blurb: The Northern Block describe Blom as a humanist sans with subtle squarish character and reverse contrast, combining heavy horizontals and modern geometry for a unique visual aesthetic. They position it as bucking the trend of conventional letterforms in favour of a versatile typeface that is inventive in style yet functional across a wide range of uses. The complete family comprises 12 fonts across six weights with matching italics.

name: Roag
classification_notes: Explicitly described as an industrial geometric sans with mechanical 1930s references; precise construction and functional character shapes place it firmly in the geometric sans category.
summary: A geometric sans with industrial 1930s mechanical references, offered in seven weights with matching italics and extensive OpenType features.
description: Built on precise geometric construction with a nod to 1930s mechanical type, Roag keeps ornament to a minimum and lets its efficient letterforms do the work. The family runs across seven weights from Thin to Black, each with a matching italic, totalling 14 fonts with over 950 characters per style. OpenType features cover eight numeral variants, small caps, stylistic alternates, ligatures, game symbols and arrows, with language support for Western, Southern and Central Europe plus Vietnamese.
foundry_blurb: The Northern Block describe Roag as an industrial geometric sans paying homage to mechanical designs of the 1930s, striking a precise balance between modern geometrics and a functional, sparing style that communicates without distraction. The family features seven weights with matching italics, over 950 characters per style, and a broad set of OpenType features including eight numeral variants, small caps, case-sensitive forms, stylistic alternates, ligatures, game symbols and arrows.

name: Syke
classification_notes: The foundry describes Syke as combining humanist and geometric concepts, with narrowly rounded letter shapes and subtle square detailing — placing it between humanist and geometric sans categories.
summary: A seven-weight sans that blends humanist warmth with geometric structure, designed for digital applications, UI and film titling.
description: Syke sits between humanist and geometric traditions, using narrowly rounded letterforms with subtle square detailing to produce a typeface that works cleanly at screen sizes and in motion contexts. Each of the seven weights carries true italics, and the family is built out with over 570 characters per style, five numeral variants, ligatures and manually edited kerning. A companion monospaced family, Syke Mono, extends the system for code or tabular use.
foundry_blurb: The Northern Block describe Syke as a versatile sans-serif combining humanist and geometric concepts, with narrowly rounded letter shapes and subtle square detailing suited to digital applications. They highlight its distinctive yet restrained character, making it a fit for film titles, user interfaces and the web, with seven weights, true italics, extensive OpenType features and a companion monospaced family.

name: Scharf
classification_notes: Scharf is a serif companion to the sans-serif Schar family, sharing its fluid and dynamic character while adding careful serif details for improved readability, placing it in the humanist serif tradition.
summary: A serif companion to the Schar sans-serif family, sharing its fluid character while adding careful detailing for editorial use.
description: Drawn as a direct companion to the sans-serif Schar, Scharf transfers that family's proportions and personality into a serifed form intended to improve readability and support complex typographic hierarchies. Sixteen fonts span eight weights from Thin to Ultra with matching true italics, and each comes with over 950 characters including alternate lowercase a, e, g and y, eight numeral variants, true small caps with accented forms and discretionary ligatures. Language coverage extends to Western, Southern and Central European scripts as well as Vietnamese.
foundry_blurb: The Northern Block describe Scharf as a sturdy serif designed as a companion to their Schar font family. Using Schar as a template, serif details are carefully drawn into each character to improve readability and enhance the font's fluid and dynamic personality. Together, the two families form an extensive type system suited to editorial design and complex typographic hierarchy.

name: Hoxton North
classification_notes: Explicitly described as a humanistic sans-serif, citing Edward Johnston and Gill Sans as primary influences, both foundational humanist sans references.
summary: A humanist sans from the north of England drawing on Edward Johnston and Gill Sans, with open forms and seven weights across 14 styles.
description: Built with reference to the London Underground typeface and Gill Sans, Hoxton North offers open letterforms and subtle stroke contrast suited to both print and screen. Seven weights run from Thin to Black, each with a true italic, and the compact proportions make it efficient at text sizes without losing presence at display scale. OpenType features include small caps, five numeral variants and coverage across Western, Southern and Central European Latin.
foundry_blurb: The Northern Block describe Hoxton North as a humanistic sans-serif created in the north of England, inspired by the modernist influences of Edward Johnston's London Underground typeface and Gill Sans. They highlight its open forms, subtle contrast and compact letterforms as qualities that make it space-efficient and versatile across headings and body text, and note its name pays homage to the creative movement in Hoxton, London, during the late 1990s.

name: Nuber Next
classification_notes: Explicitly described as a neo-grotesque sans-serif, inspired by mid-century grotesques Helvetica and Univers, with a systematic width and weight range characteristic of that tradition.
summary: A remastered neo-grotesque in nine weights and five widths, built for range and typographic consistency across contemporary design work.
description: Drawn in the tradition of Helvetica and Univers, this reworked version of the original Nuber family tightens letter shapes and introduces a five-width system spanning Condensed to Extended. Each width runs across nine weights with matching italics, giving 90 fonts in total and 750 characters per style. OpenType features include seven numeral variants, fractions, case-sensitive forms and language support for Latin and Cyrillic scripts.
foundry_blurb: The Northern Block describe Nuber Next as a modern neo-grotesque inspired by iconic 1950s typefaces such as Helvetica and Univers. Carefully remastered from the original Nuber family, it improves letter shapes and overall uniformity while adding a flexible width system. The full family covers 90 fonts with 750 characters per style, nine weights, five widths, matching italics and extensive OpenType features including Cyrillic support.

name: Ordax
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a condensed display sans-serif; its narrow proportions and weight range place it firmly in the display grotesque tradition designed for space-efficient headlines.
summary: A condensed display sans in four weights with matching italics, built for headlines where space economy and a plain, authoritative tone are priorities.
description: Eight fonts across four weights from Light to Bold, each with a matching italic, and 447 characters per style with OpenType features. The narrow proportions suit tight column measures and large-scale display work where a clean, no-frills appearance is required. Positioned as a reliable workhorse for projects that want clarity and economy over expressive personality.
foundry_blurb: The Northern Block describe Ordax as a condensed display sans-serif family designed to project reliability and gravitas. They highlight its suitability for projects demanding simplicity and space economy, with four weights and matching italics, 447 characters per style, and OpenType support across the eight-font family.

name: Zenga
classification_notes: Zenga is built on a geometric sans foundation with subtle blackletter-inspired details drawn from gothic tradition, placing it firmly in the geometric sans subcategory despite its historical references.
summary: A geometric sans with blackletter-inflected details, offered in five weights with italics and an extended character set of over 500 glyphs per style.
description: Built on clean proportions and geometric construction, Zenga introduces subtle references to gothic blackletter forms to give it a degree of historical character without sacrificing legibility. Five weights from Thin to Black each include a matching italic, and every style ships with over 500 characters, five numeral variants with stylistic zeros, ten alternate characters and chess piece symbols as part of its OpenType feature set. Suited to editorial, branding and screen work where a geometric sans needs a touch of distinctive texture.
foundry_blurb: The Northern Block describe Zenga as a contemporary typographic fusion combining geometric precision with elements drawn from blackletter and gothic tradition. They note its clean lines and exact proportions make it adaptable across print and digital contexts, and highlight its optimisation for pixel-dense screens and grid-based layouts. The family includes five weights with italics, over 500 characters per style, five numeral variations with stylistic zeros, ten alternate characters, extended symbols including chess pieces and OpenType features.

name: Typold
classification_notes: Typold is explicitly constructed from mathematical inputs with geometric forms, placing it firmly in the geometric sans-serif tradition. The foundry describes it as a modern sans serif built on mathematical principles while also incorporating humanist refinement.
summary: A geometric sans serif built across eight weights and three widths, with thorough OpenType support and a refined balance between mathematical construction and hand-drawn sensibility.
description: Typold spans 54 fonts across eight weights with matching italics and three widths, offering considerable range for display and text work alike. Details include small caps, twelve numeral variants, alternative lowercase forms for 'a' and 'y', and OpenType features covering fractions, case-sensitive punctuation and extended symbols including emojis. Language support covers Western, Southern and Central European Latin character sets.
foundry_blurb: The Northern Block describe Typold as originating from a desire to improve upon geometric forms through collaborative working methods and knowledge sharing, resulting in a modern sans serif that balances mathematical construction with the natural hand and eye of an artisan. The family includes eight weights with matching italics, three widths, 1000 characters, small caps and twelve numeral variations.

name: Borda
classification_notes: Borda is explicitly described as geometric, with precise angles, compact letterforms and a mechanical construction aimed at a futuristic aesthetic, placing it squarely in the geometric sans-serif category.
summary: A compact geometric sans with precise angles and smooth corners, built for screen-based and digital applications with a futuristic tone.
description: Six weights with matching italics give the family twelve styles, each with over 650 characters including digital numerals, fractions and tabular figures. The letterforms are tight and mechanically constructed, aimed at contexts where space efficiency and legibility at screen resolution matter. OpenType coverage extends to Western, Southern and Central European Latin as well as Extended Cyrillic.
foundry_blurb: The Northern Block describe Borda as a carefully designed geometric typeface that combines precise angles with smooth corner details for a clean and futuristic aesthetic. Its compact letterforms maximise space efficiency for digital contexts, and the family ships with six weights, italics, over 650 characters per style and broad language support including Extended Cyrillic. The font has been used by Ubisoft for Tom Clancy's The Division.

name: Brokman
classification_notes: Squarish proportions with subtle rounding and open apertures place Brokman in the geometric sans tradition, though its large x-height and fluid texture give it a contemporary, humanist-inflected quality.
summary: A seven-weight geometric sans with squarish proportions, open apertures and a large x-height, aimed at brand and digital applications.
description: Squarish forms are softened by subtle rounding, giving Brokman a modulated texture that holds up in longer text as well as display settings. Seven weights run from Thin to Bold, each with a true italic, across 430 characters with manually edited kerning and OpenType features. Noted use on Triumph Motorcycles suggests a natural fit for brand identities, broadcast and gaming contexts.
foundry_blurb: The Northern Block describe Brokman as a contemporary sans serif designed to bring clarity and originality to brand-related projects. Open apertures and a large x-height provide a fresh aesthetic with fluid texture, while squarish proportions balanced with subtle rounding give it a carefully modulated feel in longer text. Seven weights with true italics, 430 characters, manually edited kerning and OpenType features make it suitable for apps, brand identities, broadcast, gaming and the web.

name: Eund
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a geometric sans serif with minimal contrast; rectangular letterforms with smoothed curves are characteristic of geometric sans construction.
summary: A geometric sans serif with rectangular letterforms and minimal contrast, offered in nine weights with matching italics and broad character coverage including Cyrillic.
description: Rectangular forms with smoothed shallow curves give Eund a clean, precise quality suited to information-heavy applications such as data interfaces, signage and editorial systems. The family spans nine weights from Thin to Black, each with a matching italic, and each style covers over 800 characters. OpenType features include five numeral variants, fractions, alternate lowercase a and e, and coverage across Western, Southern and Central European Latin alongside Cyrillic.
foundry_blurb: The Northern Block describe Eund as a geometric sans serif with minimal contrast, where shallow curves are smoothed from rectangular letterforms to produce a fresh, legible typeface best suited to information-based applications. It includes nine weights with matching italics, over 800 characters per style, five numeral variants, OpenType alternates, and language support for Western, Southern and Central European Latin plus Cyrillic.

name: Itoya
classification_notes: A geometric sans with sharp, precise edges and a contemporary functional aesthetic, drawing from both Western geometric traditions and Japanese design sensibilities without falling into script or display territory.
summary: A geometric sans drawing on both Western precision and Japanese design thinking, available in seven weights with matching italics.
description: Itoya pairs sharp, machine-like geometry with a warmer emotional register, aiming to reflect both Western and Japanese design philosophies without resorting to pastiche. The family spans seven weights from Thin to Black, each with a matching italic, totalling 14 fonts, and covers over 600 characters per style. OpenType features include five number styles, alternate letterforms for a, e, g and y, and language support across Western, Southern and Central European Latin plus Vietnamese.
foundry_blurb: The Northern Block describe Itoya as a contemporary sans serif that blends Western and Japanese design ideologies, combining modern machine-like precision with a warm, emotional and spiritual aesthetic. They note its versatility across graphic novels, fashion and product design, and position it as a celebration of both design traditions without relying on stereotypes.

name: Kuro
classification_notes: Kuro is described as a geometric sans-serif with modern geometric forms and subtle detailing, placing it firmly in the geometric sans subcategory.
summary: A geometric sans in eight weights with subtle detailing, drawing conceptual influence from RPG video games and game theory.
description: Kuro pairs modern geometric forms with restrained detailing across eight weights, from Thin to Black, with over 450 characters per style and manually edited kerning. The lighter weights suit body copy with a contemporary feel, while the heavier cuts carry enough presence for branding, identity and packaging work. Its conceptual framing around video game culture and game theory gives it a distinct point of view without compromising its general utility.
foundry_blurb: The Northern Block describe Kuro as a precisely rendered sans-serif family combining modern geometric forms with subtle detailing for a charming, straightforward and versatile result. The design draws influence from RPG and multiple-choice games, particularly the Portal video game series, and engages with game theory as developed by John von Neumann and Oskar Morgenstern. The complete family comprises eight weights with over 450 characters per style, manually edited kerning and OpenType features.

name: Lintel
classification_notes: Lintel is described as a modern sans serif with pure, clean line forms and a proportioned, balanced structure, placing it firmly in the geometric sans tradition.
summary: A clean, balanced geometric sans in eight weights with italics, covering Latin and Cyrillic with five numeral variants and comprehensive OpenType support.
description: Designed with a pure line form and careful proportioning, Lintel aims for broad usability across editorial, branding and screen contexts. The 16-font family spans eight weights from Thin to Black, each with a matching italic, and the 500-character set per style includes five numeral styles and manually edited kerning. Latin and Cyrillic coverage extends its reach into multilingual projects.
foundry_blurb: The Northern Block describe Lintel as a modern sans serif typeface with a pure, clean line form, designed with proportioned and balanced structure for a wide variety of uses. The complete family comprises 16 fonts across eight weights with italics, featuring 500 characters per style, five numeral variations, manually edited kerning, OpenType features, and Latin and Cyrillic language support.

name: Syke Mono
classification_notes: Syke Mono is a dedicated monospaced typeface with a modern aesthetic, designed for coding and screen interfaces, placing it firmly in the mono category with a clean sans-serif construction.
summary: A modern monospaced family in five weights with italics, designed for coding, data and screen applications.
description: Initially released in 2016 and updated in 2021, Syke Mono offers ten styles across five weights with matching italics. The design distinguishes itself from traditional typewriter faces through refined details aimed at improving centre alignment and readability on screen. It suits coding environments, database interfaces and ebook applications where a modern, distinctive mono is preferable to utilitarian defaults.
foundry_blurb: The Northern Block describe Syke Mono as a modern monospaced family with a distinctive aesthetic that stands apart from the typewriter crowd. Available in 10 styles across 5 weights and italics, it features refined design details to improve centre alignment, function and readability, making it suited to coding, database applications, ebooks and screen-based interfaces. First released in 2016 and re-released in upgraded form in 2021.

name: Fagun Newspaper Specimen
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a humanist sans-serif with calligraphic roots, placing it firmly in the humanist sans subcategory distinguished by organic stroke modulation and pen-influenced letterforms.
summary: A humanist sans-serif with calligraphic roots, available in 14 styles across 7 weights with italics, covering Western, Southern and Central European languages.
description: Fagun draws on calligraphic tradition to produce a humanist sans suited to text-heavy settings, with 7 weights and matching italics giving considerable range across editorial and body copy work. Its name references Fagin from Dickens' Oliver Twist, a conceit extended across a 44-page printed newspaper specimen built around Dickensian themes. Language support spans Western, Southern and Central Europe, and the family includes an extensive set of OpenType features.
foundry_blurb: The Northern Block describe Fagun as an original humanist sans-serif design with calligraphic roots, released in 2022 in 14 styles across 7 weights with italics. The concept for the accompanying newspaper specimen is driven by Charles Dickens' novels and characters, with the name itself inspired by Fagin from Oliver Twist. They highlight the font's clarity and usability in text-based scenarios, its broad European language support and its extensive OpenType feature set.
